And there's two basic approaches to this. One is what I call roping them in, where you are pretty overtly saying the thing you want them to identify with. The classic control letter that's in the greatest sales letters of all time that starts with, if you're like me, yada, yada, you know, description. And if the person nods their head, well, yeah, I am like you, bingo, now you kind of got them. So you either do roping in language, or, and, or you make them deny negatives, which is sometimes even more effective. Because nobody likes to admit that they're in one way shape or form a loser. And so when you put that in front of them, their tendency is to deny it. So, you know, you see a lot of language in, in our kind of sales letters and ads that are, you know, this is not for everybody. And you really have to be a serious student. And you, you have to have big goals, not small goals. And you know, so that kind of language is designed to have the guy say, Oh, no, no, no, no, that's, that may not be for everybody, but I'm one of the guys that's for because no, I'm not lazy. And I'm not stupid. And I'm not cheap. And I'm not those horrible evil bad things. And so it moves them closer to you. I gave you some kind of classic examples. This is 129. This is Joe Carbo stuff. Lazy man's way to riches. And here's the challenge. Right. So what was your reaction when you read it? Did you start to reject the possibility of truth in that simple statement? Did your habit conditioned mind automatically saying not me? No way. But a wise man free of deadly self limitation would merely ask. So it's, it's, it's asking a person right there to identify themselves, either with or not with this position. And the vast majority of the people who actually are walking around burden with massive self limitation, and who immediately upon reading that sentence, said bullshit, now are challenged to react in exactly the opposite way. Oh, no, wait a minute, I'm not one of them dumb people with all those bad habits. I don't think that way, even though they do. They will tend to deny it. And now you've moved them in your, your direction. Carbo, if you go back and read the entire Lazy Man's Way to Riches book, you'll find that it was full of this stuff. This is kind of an interesting letter. I'm just going to get through it on your own time. But this is one of the Robert Collier letters actually selling a suitcase. And that's just declaration of my concepts important. But this whole direct mail campaign was built around the idea that you had to be a real smart and experienced traveler to be bright enough to appreciate this product. And the product is not for everybody. And are you this person or are you not this person? I pulled out, I didn't reproduce them. But if you want to make a note, there's exactly this kind of copy on in the preface of the time management book, you'll find it actually, you'll find this exact kind of copy almost universally at the early stages of every book I do. But it was, it's probably more pronounced in a time management book than any place else. So the first sentence of the preference is, Whimps and Willie Lohman's Beware, this book is not for the faint of heart, fawningly polite or desperate to be liked. And there's just a paragraph as a very busy, sometimes frantic, time pressured entrepreneur, a wash in opportunity too often surrounded by nitwits and slower than molasses pouring uphill folk. I understand your needs, desires and frustrations. Well, that's to get ahead not that's to let the person who picked this thing up. No, you're in the right place. I'm like you. And the person who doesn't nod their head to that really isn't right for me. But the person who does feel that way, really feels that way. And so there's a real strong immediate connection with that kind of copy. You'll find it if you go back to the business success book, I won't bother reading it to you. But if you'll look at pages 10 or 11 in that book, you'll find exactly the same kind of copy. And pretty much if you look at the beginning of any of my books, if you look at any of Napoleon Hill's books, not just Think and Grow Rich, but all of them, if you go pick up How to Win Friends and Influence People, Dale Carnegie's book, pretty much any of the classics that have endured and continued to sell 100,000 plus copies a year, year after year after year with no advertising or marketing, you're going to find one or both of these devices in their early pages. And often at the beginnings of chapters to keep reengaging the person and making them deny negatives and self identify with what's going on in that chapter. So it's a pretty powerful tactic. And it's nearly universally used. And it's applicable to salesletters, obviously, as well, as in broader purposes. And you really, it's one of those things that you really want to keep doing again and again and again, so that the people you're communicating with keep acknowledging the connection, keep saying to themselves, yeah, I'm with the right guy, I'm in the right place, that he understands me, we have this, you know, mutual appreciation for our enemies, all of that, you're doing it in a very condensed and concise form. And you're going for the external head nod and the internal sense of being understood. And it's most effective when you do it in their private language, not necessarily their public language. So, you know, most people probably aren't, for example, verbally calling the people around them nitwits and morons. But with the door closed in their office, yelling at the wall, they are calling the people around them nitwits and morons with their best buddy over a beer at the end of the day when they are complaining about what has gone on during the day, they are using very politically incorrect language. As an aside, by the way, that sort of thing is one of the many things that I fight with my book editors over tooth and nail, because it always comes back neutered into more polite language. And then we have to go argue over the fact that I don't want polite language. And you might offend people, and I do want to offend people. And I want to sound like other people who want to offend people, because that's exactly who I want to attract. So, this, I think, is a pretty important, useful and almost universally movable technique for you. It belongs in books and products, it belongs in newsletters, it belongs in weekly faxes and daily emails, as well as in sales letters and marketing materials. And even if you sell by speaking from the front of the room, for example, again, if you go back, for example, and pull the transcript to my magnetic marketing stump speech, you will find that these two devices are used at the beginning. They're used close to the bridge into the commercial, so that I'm making people identify themselves as somebody who should be buying this thing and sort of defend the fact that they are the right kind of person to buy this thing. So, this is a technique I think you want to work on some. And so, it's the first one we'll start with for the day. So, if you go to page 132, you will find a little work exercise. And I realize first thing in the morning, engaging brains isn't probably the smartest thing to do, but nevertheless, when you retreat to round tables,
